AltText: Three damp Hart’s-tongue fern leaves, photographed in black and white, photographed in ancient semi-natural woodland in Pembrokeshire Coast National Park.
Title: Fern in disguise
Description:
Among all of Britain’s ferns, Hart’s-tongue fern is probably the least fern-like. The delicate way it unfurls its leaves in spring might be the only giveaway, if you didn’t already know. If there is a less fern-like fern in Britain, then either I’ve never seen it, or else its disguise is so perfect that I didn’t suspect it of being a fern.

Anyway, full marks to the Hart’s-tongue fern for being different. Speaking of being different, I think this is the first black and white image I’ve created in over 4 years. I guess I don’t really see that way, and I usually feel that something is lost when colour is removed from a photograph, especially one of a natural subject. But in this case I felt the image — and the fern — demanded it.

The Hart’s tongue fern is considered an indicator species of ancient woodland and ecological continuity. It is not deceiving here, where I found it in ancient semi-natural woodland just outside Pembrokeshire Coast National Park.
